{"step_by_step_thinking": "Endothelin-1 (ET-1) is a potent vasoconstrictor produced by endothelial cells and plays a key role in regulating vascular tone and blood pressure. Document [1] states that ET-1 is produced from endothelial cells and acts on ETA and ETB receptors, with ETA activation increasing blood pressure. Document [8] notes that in systemic hypertension, there is a local rise in ET-1 levels in vascular walls, and ETA activation increases blood pressure, while ETB activity ameliorates it. Document [15] states that hypertension leads to increased shear stress and ET-1 production, and ET blockers have shown nephroprotective effects by reducing glomerulosclerosis and albuminuria. During hemodialysis, intradialytic hypertension and vascular resistance surges are attributed to acute changes in endothelial cell function (Document [0]). Since endothelial dysfunction is a key factor in hemodynamic instability during dialysis, and ET-1 is a major mediator of endothelial dysfunction and vasoconstriction, it is directly involved in the hemodynamic changes observed during hemodialysis. The evidence from multiple sources links ET-1 to vascular resistance, blood pressure regulation, and endothelial dysfunction—all of which are central to hemodynamic changes during dialysis. Therefore, there is a clear and supported role for endothelin-1 in these hemodynamic changes.", "answer_choice": "A"}
